and there's a concern from them if you come in, because I'm coming in, they're spending free grand a day. So I came in in a similar situation, e-commerce site, it was taking over from a previous agency, they had their very specific account set up. So I had to actually learn how their account was set up and have to change my processes and my kind of reports and everything to fit in with that. It was an absolute nightmare. It was two to three months of just struggling to get the account working. It wasn't how I'm used to working. I had to kind of operate in a different mindset of how the traffic and funnels were set up. And with hindsight, I made a big mistake in not going in and saying, yes, I will continue some of it running, but I'm going to migrate it over to the way I run it. So my recommendation would be to get it into a position that you're comfortable with as soon as possible. At the same time, you don't want to lose performance. So I'd cut the ad sets and campaigns that are not working and start shifting stuff over to your new format. Now, whether it's in the same account, in which case just put like your agency initials at the front of your campaigns just to keep it clear, or whether you go into a separate campaign, that's entirely a separate account, that's up to you. But I would slowly look to gradually to kind of move that over to an account structure that makes more sense to you because just down the line, you'll end up hitting kind of barriers where you just don't understand why the previous agency did XYZ. If you're not getting any clicks on a particular ad, does it mean that the ad doesn't work? Again, that's something I would cycle my ads to test, whether it was that specific ad. Or if I'm consistently seeing it on an audience that I think should be engaged, then I might make a different decision and test it in website conversion anyway. What is the best practice for... If there's site downtime, is it better to switch off your ads or... 100%? Yeah. ...before you leave it running? 100% turn it off. And the reason is that when the site is down, the pixel is not sending anything back to Facebook, Facebook will screw up your ad sets because it thinks that the traffic is no good and the traffic is bouncing and all that kind of stuff. When in reality, it's nothing to do with Facebook. So I've seen this so many times where the site is down, you might pause your ads after an hour, two hours, but by that time, the damage has been done because Facebook starts to adjust its algorithm because it thinks that it's not getting positive signals back like the purchases or whatever from your website starts to adjust its targeting. By the time you restart it and the website is back to normal, then the good audiences, they're all over the place. So if your site is down, I would switch all the campaigns off.
